channel.title: ChemBioChem (no repeats)
channel.description: Table of Contents for ChemBioChem. List of articles from both the latest and EarlyView issues.
channel.dc:title: ChemBioChem (no repeats)

item.title: The Keto Functions of Heme d1 Are Introduced by NirF and NirC
item.dc:description:
During heme d1 biosynthesis, the proteins NirF and NirC together catalyze the formation of dihydro‐heme d1, thereby introducing the unique keto functions of heme d1. In this paper, the development of an in vitro enzyme activity assay for NirF/NirC is described, which demonstrates the function of the two proteins for the first time.
The modified tetrapyrrole heme d1 plays an important role for bacterial denitrification as essential cofactor of the cytochrome cd1 nitrite reductase. Structurally, heme d1 is a dioxo‐isobacteriochlorin carrying two keto functions on pyrrole rings A and B. While most of the steps of heme d1 biosynthesis were elucidated, the introduction of the keto functions remained enigmatic. In this study, we show that the proteins NirF and NirC catalyze the formation of the keto functions. NirF binds the nonenzymatically formed dilactone form of 3,8‐dideoxo‐dihydro‐heme d1 and together with NirC transforms this intermediate into dihydro‐heme d1 as shown by HPLC‐UV/Vis and HPLC‐MS analysis. The additional oxygen atoms of dihydro‐heme d1 derive from water based on experiments performed with 18O‐labeled H2O.
item.content:encoded:
During heme d 1 biosynthesis, the proteins NirF and NirC together catalyze the formation of dihydro-heme d 1, thereby introducing the unique keto functions of heme d 1. In this paper, the development of an in vitro enzyme activity assay for NirF/NirC is described, which demonstrates the function of the two proteins for the first time.


The modified tetrapyrrole heme d 1 plays an important role for bacterial denitrification as essential cofactor of the cytochrome cd 1 nitrite reductase. Structurally, heme d 1 is a dioxo-isobacteriochlorin carrying two keto functions on pyrrole rings A and B. While most of the steps of heme d 1 biosynthesis were elucidated, the introduction of the keto functions remained enigmatic. In this study, we show that the proteins NirF and NirC catalyze the formation of the keto functions. NirF binds the nonenzymatically formed dilactone form of 3,8-dideoxo-dihydro-heme d 1 and together with NirC transforms this intermediate into dihydro-heme d 1 as shown by HPLC-UV/Vis and HPLC-MS analysis. The additional oxygen atoms of dihydro-heme d 1 derive from water based on experiments performed with 18O-labeled H2O.
item.dc:creator: Pia Hebecker, Gunhild Layer
item.dc:title: The Keto Functions of Heme d1 Are Introduced by NirF and NirC
